g.data-narration: Welcome to Distributed Memory Systems. We'll explore how large-scale parallel computers organize memory and communicate between processors.
In distributed-memory systems, each processor has its own private local memory. Processors cannot directly access another processor's memory space.
g.text: Each processor operates on data in its
own local memory — no global address space exists.

g.data-narration: Why does this architecture matter? It scales to thousands of cores, powers supercomputers, and forms the backbone of modern cloud computing clusters.
g.text: Used for weather forecasting, genome sequencing, physics simulations, and AI training.

g.data-narration: Here's the hardware organization: multiple independent nodes, each with CPU and local memory, connected through a high-speed interconnection network.
Three key characteristics define distributed memory: private address spaces, explicit message passing communication, and high scalability through additional nodes.
Processors communicate by explicitly sending and receiving messages. Here's how data moves from Node 0 to Node 1 over the network.
Distributed memory uses MPI (Message Passing Interface). Programmers explicitly decompose problems, partition data, and define send/receive communication points.
Distributed memory offers three key advantages: massive scalability, no cache coherence overhead, and cost-effective commodity hardware construction.
The key challenges are communication overhead from network latency and bandwidth limits, plus the complexity of explicit data distribution and load balancing.

g.text: Network latency and bandwidth can become performance bottlenecks
Manual data decomposition and load balancing required

g.data-narration: Modern HPC systems use hybrid architectures: distributed memory between nodes, shared memory within nodes, combining MPI and OpenMP programming models.
In summary: distributed memory has private address spaces per processor, uses explicit message passing via MPI, scales to millions of cores, and powers modern supercomputers.
g.text: • Private address spaces → No global memory access
